note: v2 3-layer model: pre-upload text features → post-upload YouTube metrics → log10(views). All pre-upload features are quantifiable: pure text statistics or closed grammatical categories. Arbitrary phrase-list indicators were removed and are expected to re-emerge as compound features.

pre_nodes.label: Transcript Word Count
pre_nodes.description: Total word count of the windowed text.
pre_nodes.algorithm: len(text.split()) — counts whitespace-separated tokens.
pre_nodes.quantifiable_reason: Pure tokenization. No vocabulary, no domain knowledge.

pre_nodes.label: Unique Word Ratio
pre_nodes.description: Type–token ratio: unique words divided by total words.
pre_nodes.algorithm: len(set(words)) / len(words). Standard type–token ratio.
pre_nodes.quantifiable_reason: Pure math on word identities. No domain knowledge.

pre_nodes.label: Hapax Legomena Ratio
pre_nodes.description: Fraction of words that appear exactly once (hapax legomena).
pre_nodes.algorithm: count(w for w in words if words.count(w) == 1) / len(words). A standard corpus-linguistics metric.
pre_nodes.quantifiable_reason: Counts singletons — a closed mathematical operation. No vocabulary required.

pre_nodes.label: Pivot Word Count
pre_nodes.description: Count of contrastive conjunctions (a closed grammatical category in English).
pre_nodes.algorithm: Counts whole-word, case-insensitive matches of the closed grammatical category of contrastive conjunctions: ['but', 'however', 'yet', 'although', 'whereas', 'while', 'nevertheless', 'meanwhile', 'despite', 'instead', 'rather', 'conversely', 'nonetheless', 'on the other hand', 'in contrast']. These are defined by English grammar (Quirk et al., 1985, §13.30+), not by arbitrary topic selection.
pre_nodes.quantifiable_reason: Contrastive conjunctions are a CLOSED grammatical class in English — defined by syntactic function, not by topic. Membership is fixed by the language, not curated.

pre_nodes.label: Pivot Word Density
pre_nodes.description: Contrastive conjunctions per 100 words — length-normalized.
pre_nodes.algorithm: (pivot_word_count / max(word_count, 1)) × 100. Density per 100 words.
pre_nodes.quantifiable_reason: Same closed grammatical class as pivot_word_count, normalized for text length.

pre_nodes.label: Comparison Word Count
pre_nodes.description: Count of comparative markers (a closed grammatical class).
pre_nodes.algorithm: Counts whole-word matches of comparative markers: ['than', 'vs', 'versus', 'more', 'less', 'better', 'worse', 'greater', 'smaller', 'higher', 'lower', 'faster', 'slower']. These signal grammatical comparison constructions.
pre_nodes.quantifiable_reason: 'than', 'vs', 'versus' are closed-class function words. The comparative adjectives ('more', 'less', '-er' forms) are part of the comparison construction grammar, not topic vocabulary.

pre_nodes.label: Hook Word Ratio
pre_nodes.description: Fraction of interrogative wh-words and inversion auxiliaries.
pre_nodes.algorithm: count(w in {'what','how','why','who','when','where','which','whose','will','can','could','would','should','do','does','did','is','are','was','were','if','whether'}) / len(words). Closed grammatical class of interrogatives + auxiliaries used in question formation.
pre_nodes.quantifiable_reason: WH-words and inversion auxiliaries form a closed grammatical class for question formation in English. Membership is fixed by the language.

pre_nodes.quantifiable_reason: Second-person pronouns are a closed grammatical class in English. Five entries, fixed by the language.

pre_nodes.label: Hook Question Count

pre_nodes.algorithm: Counts occurrences of '?' in the original (case-preserving) text.
pre_nodes.quantifiable_reason: Counts a single punctuation glyph. No vocabulary, no domain knowledge.

pre_nodes.label: Repeated Phrase Count
pre_nodes.description: Number of bigrams that repeat 2+ times with at least 10 words between occurrences.
pre_nodes.algorithm: Build all bigrams. For each bigram with 2+ occurrences, check if any consecutive pair of occurrences is separated by ≥10 word positions. Count bigrams that satisfy this. No phrase list — the bigrams come from the text itself.
pre_nodes.quantifiable_reason: Pure structural detection of self-repetition with positional gap. No vocabulary or topic knowledge.

post_nodes.label: Swipe-Away Rate
post_nodes.description: Fraction of viewers who swipe away in the first second. Lower = better. Source: YouTube analytics swiped_away_rate_pct.
post_nodes.improve_hint: Front-load contrastive conjunctions (pivot_word) and questions in the @1s window — give the viewer something to resolve.

post_nodes.label: End Recovery Score
post_nodes.description: Mean retention from 80–95% of the video. Captures whether viewers "stick the landing".
post_nodes.improve_hint: Set up bigram callbacks (repeated_phrase_count) early — they pay off in the back half.

post_nodes.label: Retention Q4/Q1 Ratio
post_nodes.description: Mean retention in last quarter divided by first quarter. >1 = curve rising, <1 = curve falling.
post_nodes.improve_hint: Use repeated_phrase callbacks and pivot_word transitions to make the back half feel earned.

post_nodes.label: Final-Quarter Retention
post_nodes.description: Mean retention across the final quarter of the video (0.75–1.0).
post_nodes.improve_hint: Higher transcript_word_count and pivot_word density correlate with stronger final-quarter retention.

post_nodes.label: Hook Drop Rate
post_nodes.description: Retention slope across the first 20% of the video (negative = dropping fast). Less negative = better hook.
post_nodes.improve_hint: Reduce hook_word_ratio (over-using question words hurts), favor contrastive conjunctions and concrete tokens.

post_nodes.label: Avg % Viewed
post_nodes.description: Average percentage of the video each viewer watched.
post_nodes.improve_hint: Longer hook setup (transcript_word_count @5s) and structural pivots correlate with higher overall retention.

removed_indicators.reason: 31 hand-curated phrases. Should emerge as a compound of action verbs + first-person + past tense.
30+ hand-curated phrases. Should emerge from interrogatives + future modals + uncertainty markers.
26 hand-curated words. Should emerge from concrete-noun classifiers, not a fixed list.
40 hand-curated verbs. Should emerge from POS tagging + verb-class clusters.
10 hand-curated sentence starters. Should emerge from discourse-marker analysis.
Overlaps pivot_word but uses different curated phrases. Not independently grammatical.
Counts presence across multiple curated families. Compound of arbitrary lists.
Position of first match against curated escalation phrases. Arbitrary.
Definition of 'loop' itself depends on curated phrase lists.
